summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common wisdom that 'shipping is enough' by arguing that the true measure of a programmer's success is building software that people actually use. He quotes Chuck Jazdzewski's advice that programmers get paid to ship, but pushes further to question the value of shipping code that nobody will ever use or benefit from. Through Rich Skrenta's experience of realizing his side projects had more users than his day job at a major company, Atwood illustrates how tragically common it is for programmers to work on code that will never matter. He concludes that smart developers understand their job encompasses far more than coding—including documentation, interaction design, and cultivating user community—because without getting those right, the code itself becomes meaningless.
key_points: The ultimate metric of programmer success isn't shipping code, but how many users actually use your application
Many programmers at major companies spend their careers writing code that will never be used or noticed—which is as tragic as writing a blog nobody reads
Smart developers recognize that their job is to build software worth using, not just to write and ship code
Non-coding activities like documentation, interaction design, and user community cultivation are critical to software success
If you want to work on software people use, you must take responsibility for making that software worth using
money_quotes: You don't get paid to program, you get paid to ship.
How many users actually use your application? Now that's the ultimate metric of success.
So I made a simple resolution. I wanted to work on stuff that people would actually use.
What about the legion of programmers writing code paths that will never be traversed?
A smart software developer knows that there's no point in writing code if it's code that nobody will see, code that nobody will use, code that nobody will ultimately benefit from.
Why build a permanently vacant house?
Their job is to build software that people will actually want to use.
If you get that stuff wrong, it won't matter what kind of code you've written.
key_insight: The true measure of a programmer's success isn't shipping code—it's building software that people actually want to use, which requires taking ownership of everything that makes software worth using.
